# Transcrição: 11-Small Freezing Children

- **Fonte:** 11-Small Freezing Children
- **Duração:** 06:37
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:01]

And let's keep it going for Matthew. Thank you. Thank you. Got it. I'm staring at my children, Clara and Charlie, and I'm screaming at them. I am hollering at the top of my lungs. I am so angry at them. Clara is a 10-year-old militant feminist who comes down in the morning and asks me questions like, do you know who Madam C.J. Walker is? And if I don't know, I catch hell. But she's also a kid who can walk into her room and jump over a mountain of wet towels like they don't exist. She's a monster. And Charlie is seven, and he's funny. He's legitimately hilarious. 10% of his jokes make me laugh. And that's a good percentage for a seven-year-old comedian. But Charlie is in love with my wife, his mother. And he proposes to her daily. And just a couple weeks ago, he told my wife, I have a girlfriend, an attempt to drive a wedge between us. I do not have a girlfriend. And so I don't trust Charlie anymore. And so I'm yelling at them for this transgression they are committing, and I can't believe it. But really, the reason I'm yelling at them in this particular way is because of 40 boys at a camp, a YMCA camp 10 years earlier. It's 2009, and I'm at a YMCA camp in Connecticut. I'm a fifth grade teacher, and we take all of our children there every year. And it's normally a fall trip, but a hurricane that turned into some wind canceled the trip, so we had to go in the winter. So there's 12 feet of snow and it's negative three degrees. And I have been in a cabin for two days with 40 boys, and they are killing me. I spend every day saying, would you act like a fifth grader? When in my head I'm thinking you're acting exactly like a fifth grader. That is the problem. These are boys who like leap off bunks to try to break as many bones as possible. Jesus. These are boys who emit fluids from every orifice and an attempt to make each other laugh. And they won't sleep. It's been two days and they will not sleep. I have 40 boys with two men in the cabin because I'm an elementary school teacher. On the other side of the pond, my pregnant wife is also a teacher, and there's 40 girls, but there's 17 women, and they're doing braiding and beating and kumbaya. And I'm stuck with one guy and 40 boys. And the guy I have is named Paul. And Paul is if someone made someone who is the opposite of me, that is Paul. He is a vegan from California. He's got the Grateful Dead concert just playing in his head all the time. He once asked me if my pants were made from organic cotton, and it took me two days to stop laughing at that question. And all he does is chill while all these boys make me crazy.

## [03:01 - 06:03]

And so I can't take it anymore. So I tell them you're going to have s'mores, and then you're going to go to bed. I try to bribe them with food, which always works, but the equation here is wrong because what you normally do is you get kids to do something, and then you feed them. But I can't get them to go to sleep and then jam s'mores into their mouth. So I have to take their word that they will sleep, which is a mistake, because fifth graders are liars. And so I feed them the s'mores and they don't go to bed. So I get so angry. I stand up on a coffee table and I say, go to bed. Go into that room and close the door, and I don't want to see you till the morning. And they do. They all run in this common room where Paul and I are sleeping, is finally quiet, and we go to bed. In the morning I wake up and I'm so hot, I think the cabin is on fire. And I'm on a top bunk, so I call down to Paul. I say, Paul, are you hot? And he goes, oh my God, man, it's so hot. And then I hear the knocking on the door where the boys are sleeping. And I say, what? And the door opens and a boy pokes his head in and he starts to speak. I will never forget what I see. I see his breath. And then I notice the winter cap he has on and the mittens. As he's talking, I can see his breath. And he says, can I come in? And I can see he's shivering. And I say, yes, come in. What is your problem? And he opens the door and a blast of frigid air hits me. And I say, Jesus, what are you doing? I think they've broken the window. He's dressed all the way in all of his winter gear. And I'll later find out he has all of his clothes on. He's put every single thing he has on. And a trail of boys all wearing all of their winter gear start coming into this common room. And they're all shivering and white. I'll find out later they didn't sleep at all. They got out of their bunks in the middle of the night. They made a pile of boys in the middle of the room and cuddled all night long to stay warm. And I look and I realize what I've done. The heat source from this room, which is a fall cabin, comes out of our room and goes into their room. And so when I forced them to close the door, I cut them off of heat. And in the middle of the night, they froze. And so I stoke the fire and I try to thaw them out as quickly as I can. And that morning, I meet with my colleagues and a female teacher says, you know, I heard what happened to the boys last night. They sure learned their lesson. Like, they're never going to do that to you again. And my thought is immediately that that's the convenient answer. That is the simple way of looking at it. But what really happened was I frightened a bunch of boys who were far away from home. And in the middle of the night, when they were very cold, they didn't have anyone to turn to. And they should have been able to ask me for help. And that's why I'm screaming at my own children right now. Because I told them to take their bathing suits off while I packed the car and they didn't. And they're on the picnic table in their bathing suits. So I'm in my car with the doors closed and I'm screaming at them.

## [06:03 - 06:37]

Because that's how I yell at my kids. I go far away from them and then I yell at them so they can't hear me. Because every parent has to scream at their children. But they don't have to do it in the children's presence. And so truthfully, I've never raised my voice to my kids. Not because I'm a good person or I'm a great parent, truly. But because 40 boys who froze one night in a cabin in Connecticut taught me that we should discipline kids, but we should not frighten them while we're doing it. Thank you.
